qtype: algebraic manipulation, indices, algebraic fractions, linear equation
tex:
(a) % (a)
    (i) (b) Write 70 as a product of its prime factors.
\newline
 

        

(c) Find the highest common factor (HCF) of 70 and 112.
\newline
 

        

(d) Find the lowest common multiple (LCM) of $70x^4 y^2$ and $112x^3 y^5$.
\newline
 

        

(e) % (b)
    Simplify.
\newline
    (i) (f) $a^{12} \div a^4$ 

        

(g) $\dfrac{5}{2b} \times \dfrac{bc}{20}$ 

        

(h) % (c)
    Solve.
\newline
    \[ 4 + 2x = 15 \]

    

(i) % (d)
    Solve.
\newline
    \[ \dfrac{34 + 2x}{5} = 4 - x \]

    

(j) % (e)
    $P = d + \sqrt[3]{m^2}$
    (i) (k) Find $P$ when $d = 7$ and $m = -8$.
\newline
 

        

(l) Rearrange the formula to make $m$ the subject.
\newline

content.content:
Write 70 as a product of its prime factors.
\newline

Find the highest common factor (HCF) of 70 and 112.
\newline

Find the lowest common multiple (LCM) of $70x^4 y^2$ and $112x^3 y^5$.
\newline

% (b)
    Simplify.
\newline
$\dfrac{5}{2b} \times \dfrac{bc}{20}$

% (c)
    Solve.
\newline
    \[ 4 + 2x = 15 \]

% (d)
    Solve.
\newline
    \[ \dfrac{34 + 2x}{5} = 4 - x \]

Find $P$ when $d = 7$ and $m = -8$.
\newline

Rearrange the formula to make $m$ the subject.
\newline

tex:
(a) On the grid, draw % (a)
    (i) (b) the image of triangle $A$ after a reflection in the line $x = 1$.
\newline
 

(c) the image of triangle $A$ after an enlargement by scale factor $\dfrac{1}{2}$ with centre $(5, 1)$.
\newline
 

(d) Describe fully the single transformation that maps triangle $A$ onto triangle $B$.
\newline
 

    

(e) The point $(a, b)$ is reflected in the line $y = k$ where $k$ is an integer and $b \neq k$.
\newline
 \\
    Write the coordinates of the image of point $(a, b)$ in terms of $a, b$ and $k$.
\newline
 

     (, )

tex:
(a) The table shows the waiting times for 120 patients at a medical centre.
\newline
 % (a)

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}
        \begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{|L|C|C|C|C|C|}
        \hline
        Waiting time ($t$ minutes) & $0 < t \leqslant 10$ & $10 < t \leqslant 20$ & $20 < t \leqslant 40$ & $40 < t \leqslant 50$ & $50 < t \leqslant 80$ \\
        \hline
        Frequency & 2 & 46 & 33 & 26 & 13 \\
        \hline
        \end{tabularx}
    \end{InsertScreenShot}

    Calculate an estimate of the mean waiting time.
\newline
 

    

(b) The histogram shows some information about the waiting times at a different medical centre.
\newline
 % (b)

    The total number of patients is 90 and no patient waits for more than 80 minutes.
\newline
 \\
    Complete the histogram for the patients that have a waiting time between 10 and 30 minutes.
\newline

content.content:
The table shows the waiting times for 120 patients at a medical centre.
\newline
 % (a)

Calculate an estimate of the mean waiting time.
\newline

The histogram shows some information about the waiting times at a different medical centre.
\newline
 % (b)

The total number of patients is 90 and no patient waits for more than 80 minutes.
\newline
 \\
    Complete the histogram for the patients that have a waiting time between 10 and 30 minutes.
\newline

tex:
(a) % (a)
    Enzo, Rashid and Blessy each swim as many lengths of a swimming pool as they can in 15 minutes.
\newline
 \\
    The results are shown in the table.
\newline

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}
        \begin{tabular}{|l|c|}
        \hline
        Name & Number of lengths \\
        \hline
        Enzo & 11.25 \\
        Rashid & 18.75 \\
        Blessy & 20 \\
        \hline
        \end{tabular}
\end{InsertScreenShot}

    (i) (b) Find the number of lengths Enzo swims as a percentage of the total number of lengths all three people swim.
\newline
 

        

(c) Write the ratio of the number of lengths each person swims in the form
        \[ Enzo : Rashid : Blessy \]
        Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline
 

        

(d) Each length of the pool is 25 m.
\newline
        \begin{subsubparts} % Level 3 numbering (a), (b)

(e) Work out Blessy's average swimming speed for the 15 minutes.
\newline
 \\
            Give your answer in metres per second.
\newline
 

            

(f) Rashid continues to swim at the same rate.
\newline
 \\
            Calculate the time it takes Rashid to swim a total distance of 5 km.
\newline
 \\
            Give your answer in hours and minutes.
\newline

             ]

        \end{subsubparts}

(g) Blessy swims for one hour.
\newline
 \\
        The number of lengths she swims decreases by 5\% every 15 minutes.
\newline
 \\
        Calculate the number of lengths she swims in the final 15 minutes.
\newline
 

        

(h) % (b)
    Another swimmer, Adam, swims 450 m, correct to the nearest 25 metres.
\newline
 \\
    This takes 10 minutes, correct to the nearest minute.
\newline
 \\
    Calculate the minimum distance Adam swims in one hour at this rate.
\newline

content.content:
% (a)
    Enzo, Rashid and Blessy each swim as many lengths of a swimming pool as they can in 15 minutes.
\newline
 \\
    The results are shown in the table.
\newline

Find the number of lengths Enzo swims as a percentage of the total number of lengths all three people swim.
\newline

Write the ratio of the number of lengths each person swims in the form
        \[ \text{Enzo} : \text{Rashid} : \text{Blessy} \]
        Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

Each length of the pool is 25 m.
\newline

Work out Blessy's average swimming speed for the 15 minutes. \\
            Give your answer in metres per second.

Rashid continues to swim at the same rate. \\
            Calculate the time it takes Rashid to swim a total distance of 5 km. \\
            Give your answer in hours and minutes.\par

Blessy swims for one hour.
\newline
 \\
        The number of lengths she swims decreases by 5\% every 15 minutes.
\newline
 \\
        Calculate the number of lengths she swims in the final 15 minutes.
\newline

% (b)
    Another swimmer, Adam, swims 450 m, correct to the nearest 25 metres.
\newline
 \\
    This takes 10 minutes, correct to the nearest minute.
\newline
 \\
    Calculate the minimum distance Adam swims in one hour at this rate.
\newline

tex:
A box contains 3 blue pens and 5 red pens.
\newline

(a) Mia picks a pen from the box at random.
\newline
 \\ % (a)
    Find the probability that she picks a red pen.
\newline
 

    

(b) Mia puts the pen back into the box.
\newline
 \\ % (b)
    She then picks a pen at random and replaces it.
\newline
 \\
    She then picks a second pen at random.
\newline
    (i) (c) Complete the tree diagram.
\newline
 

        

(d) Find the probability that Mia picks two pens that have the same colour.
\newline
 

        

(e) Mia now picks 3 of the 8 pens in the box at random without replacement.
\newline
 \\ % (c)
    Find the probability that she picks 2 blue pens and 1 red pen.
\newline

content.content:
Mia picks a pen from the box at random.
\newline
 \\ % (a)
    Find the probability that she picks a red pen.
\newline

Mia puts the pen back into the box.
\newline
 \\ % (b)
    She then picks a pen at random and replaces it.
\newline
 \\
    She then picks a second pen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that Mia picks two pens that have the same colour.
\newline

Mia now picks 3 of the 8 pens in the box at random without replacement.
\newline
 \\ % (c)
    Find the probability that she picks 2 blue pens and 1 red pen.
\newline

stem.content:
A box contains 3 blue pens and 5 red pens.
\newline

tex:
The diagram shows a field $ABCD$.
\newline
 

A straight path $AC$ goes across the field.
\newline

$AD = 420$ m, $DC = 830$ m and $AB = 1150$ m.
\newline
 

Angle $ADC = 106^\circ$ and angle $CAB = 62^\circ$.
\newline

(a) Show that $AC = 1028$ m, correct to the nearest metre.
\newline
 

    

(b) Angle $ACB$ is obtuse.
\newline
 \\
    Calculate angle $ACB$.
\newline
 

    

(c) Part of the field, triangle $ACD$, is sold for $\$41\,500$.
\newline
 \\
    Calculate the cost of 1 hectare of this part of the field.
\newline
 \\
    Give your answer correct to the nearest dollar.
\newline
 \\
    $[1  hectare = 10\,000  m^2]$

\newline

 \\

\newline
 \\
    Calculate the cost of 1 hectare of this part of the field.
\newline
 \\
    Give your answer correct to the nearest dollar.
\newline
 \\
    $[1 \text{ hectare} = 10\,000 \text{ m}^2]$

The diagram shows a field $ABCD$.
\newline
 \par
A straight path $AC$ goes across the field.
\newline

$AD = 420$ m, $DC = 830$ m and $AB = 1150$ m.
\newline
 \par
Angle $ADC = 106^\circ$ and angle $CAB = 62^\circ$.
\newline

tex:
A company makes scientific calculators and graphic calculators.
\newline
 

Each day they make $x$ scientific calculators and $y$ graphic calculators.
\newline

These inequalities describe the number of scientific and graphic calculators they make each day.
\newline
\[ x \geqslant 180 \qquad y \leqslant 90 \qquad x + y \leqslant 240 \]

(a) Complete these two statements.
\newline
 
 % (a)
    The company makes fewer than \BlankInText{} scientific calculators each day.
\newline
 \\
    The company can make a maximum of \BlankInText{} calculators each day.
\newline
 

(b) Scientific calculators cost $\$12$ to make.
\newline
 \\ % (b)
    Graphic calculators cost $\$18$ to make.
\newline
 \\
    Each day the company spends at least $\$2700$ making calculators.
\newline
 \\
    Show that $2x + 3y \geqslant 450$.
\newline
 

    

(c) The region $R$ satisfies these four inequalities.
\newline
 % (c)
    \[ x \geqslant 180 \qquad y \leqslant 90 \qquad x + y \leqslant 240 \qquad 2x + 3y \geqslant 450 \]
    By drawing four suitable lines and shading unwanted regions on the grid, find and label the region $R$.
\newline

    

(d) Scientific calculators are sold for a profit of $\$10$.
\newline
 \\ % (d)
    Graphic calculators are sold for a profit of $\$30$.
\newline
 \\
    Calculate the maximum profit made by the company in one day.
\newline

content.content:
Complete these two statements.
\newline
 \par % (a)
    The company makes fewer than \BlankInText{} scientific calculators each day.
\newline
 \\
    The company can make a maximum of \BlankInText{} calculators each day.
\newline

Scientific calculators cost $\$12$ to make.
\newline
 \\ % (b)

\newline
 \\
    Each day the company spends at least $\$2700$ making calculators.
\newline
 \\
    Show that $2x + 3y \geqslant 450$.
\newline

The region $R$ satisfies these four inequalities.
\newline
 % (c)
    \[ x \geqslant 180 \qquad y \leqslant 90 \qquad x + y \leqslant 240 \qquad 2x + 3y \geqslant 450 \]
    By drawing four suitable lines and shading unwanted regions on the grid, find and label the region $R$.
\newline

Scientific calculators are sold for a profit of $\$10$.

 \\ % (d)

stem.content:
A company makes scientific calculators and graphic calculators.
\newline
 \par
Each day they make $x$ scientific calculators and $y$ graphic calculators.
\newline


These inequalities describe the number of scientific and graphic calculators they make each day.
\newline
\[ x \geqslant 180 \qquad y \leqslant 90 \qquad x + y \leqslant 240 \]

_rawBody:
A company makes scientific calculators and graphic calculators. \par
Each day they make $x$ scientific calculators and $y$ graphic calculators.


These inequalities describe the number of scientific and graphic calculators they make each day.
\[ x \geqslant 180 \qquad y \leqslant 90 \qquad x + y \leqslant 240 \]


\begin{parts}
    \item Complete these two statements. \par % (a)
    The company makes fewer than \BlankInText{} scientific calculators each day. \\
    The company can make a maximum of \BlankInText{} calculators each day. 
    \Marks{2}

    \item Scientific calculators cost $\$12$ to make. \\ % (b)
    Graphic calculators cost $\$18$ to make. \\
    Each day the company spends at least $\$2700$ making calculators. \\
    Show that $2x + 3y \geqslant 450$. 
    \answerlines[1]
    \Marks{1}

    \item The region $R$ satisfies these four inequalities. % (c)
    \[ x \geqslant 180 \qquad y \leqslant 90 \qquad x + y \leqslant 240 \qquad 2x + 3y \geqslant 450 \]
    By drawing four suitable lines and shading unwanted regions on the grid, find and label the region $R$.
    \relinput{Figures/Q07-Grid.tex}
    \Marks{7}

    \item Scientific calculators are sold for a profit of $\$10$. \\ % (d)
    Graphic calculators are sold for a profit of $\$30$. \\
    Calculate the maximum profit made by the company in one day. 
    \AnswerLine[$\$$][]
    \Marks{2}
\end{parts}

qtype: solving quadratic equations, inverse functions, composite functions, sketching graphs, differentiation for tangent
tex:
(a) $f(x) = 7 - 3x$ \qquad $g(x) = x^2 - 16$ 
 % (a) is complex, using subparts
    (i) (b) Find the values of $x$ when $g(x) = 20$.
\newline
 

         ]

(c) Find $f^{-1}(x)$.
\newline
 

        

(d) Find $gf(x) + 1$, giving your answer in its simplest form.
\newline
 

        

(e) On the axes, sketch the graph of $y = g(x)$.
\newline
 

        On your sketch, indicate the values where the graph crosses the axes.
\newline

        

(f) Find the equation of the tangent to the graph of $y = g(x)$ when $x = -3$.
\newline
 

        Give your answer in the form $y = mx + c$.
\newline
 

        

(g) % (b)
    $h(x) = 3^x$
    (i) (h) On the axes, sketch the graph of $y = h(x)$.
\newline
 

        

(i) Write down the equation of the asymptote to the graph of $y = h(x)$.
\newline

content.content: $f(x) = 7 - 3x$ \qquad $g(x) = x^2 - 16$ \par % (a) is complex, using subparts

Find the values of $x$ when $g(x) = 20$.
\newline
 \par

\newline

Find $gf(x) + 1$, giving your answer in its simplest form.
\newline
 \par

\newline
 \par
        On your sketch, indicate the values where the graph crosses the axes.
\newline
\par

Find the equation of the tangent to the graph of $y = g(x)$ when $x = -3$.
\newline

\newline

\newline

\newline

tex:
$ABCDEFGH$ is a solid cuboid.
\newline
 

$AB = 10$ cm, $BC = 8$ cm and $CG = 17$ cm.
\newline

(a) Work out the volume of the cuboid.
\newline
 

(b) Work out the total surface area of the cuboid.
\newline
 

(c) Calculate the angle between $GA$ and the base $ABCD$.
\newline
 

(d) A straight rod $PQ$ is placed inside the cuboid.
\newline
 

One end of the rod, $P$, is placed at the midpoint of $AB$.
\newline
 

The other end of the rod, $Q$, rests on $GH$.
\newline
 

$HQ : QG = 4 : 1$.
\newline

Calculate the length of the rod $PQ$.
\newline

content.content:
Work out the volume of the cuboid.
\newline
 \par

Work out the total surface area of the cuboid.
\newline
 \par

Calculate the angle between $GA$ and the base $ABCD$.
\newline
 \par

A straight rod $PQ$ is placed inside the cuboid.
\newline
 \par
One end of the rod, $P$, is placed at the midpoint of $AB$.
\newline
 \par
The other end of the rod, $Q$, rests on $GH$.
\newline
 \par
$HQ : QG = 4 : 1$.
\newline
\par

Calculate the length of the rod $PQ$.
\newline
 \par

stem.content:
$ABCDEFGH$ is a solid cuboid.
\newline
 \par
$AB = 10$ cm, $BC = 8$ cm and $CG = 17$ cm.
\newline
